fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get this binary serialization thing shipped. The basic idea is we have structured data (records with various field types) and we want to convert them to bytes and back again reliably. Think of it like our own compact wire format — similar to what we did with that encoding layer in the transport module, but more self-contained.

The tricky parts are: we need to handle ALL the primitive types including the weird ones like dates (stored as ticks, similar to how the calendar service does it), GUIDs in that mixed-endian layout we've used before, and those 64-bit integers that JS can't represent natively so they come in as strings. We also need nested stuff — maps, arrays, unions with discriminators, enumerations with different backing sizes.

There's also this decode-only operation where someone hands us a pre-baked hex blob and we need to parse it into a known shape (songs catalog format, I think it was spec'd somewhere in the test fixtures). Oh and constants/flags resolution needs to work too — the bit-flag enum has some combined members and even a negative one, make sure those compute correctly.

The output format for decoded records needs to be deterministic — fields in alphabetical order, maps in wire order. Floats have special string representations for infinity/NaN. Please just refer to how the existing round-trip tests expect the output to look, that's the contract.

One more pass on the details the team asked about so we stay aligned with what the tests already expect. For dates, we’re storing them as 8-byte little-endian unsigned integers representing 100-nanosecond ticks since 0001-01-01. The formula is: ticks = (milliseconds_since_unix_epoch * 10000 + 621355968000000000) bitwise-AND with 0x3fffffffffffffff (top two bits cleared). Decoding reverses this: milliseconds = (ticks - 621355968000000000) / 10000. Same thing stated the other way from the wire rules: ticks = milliseconds * 10000 + 621355968000000000, then masked with 0x3fffffffffffffff. This is the .NET DateTime ticks epoch offset.

For GUIDs, we do want the canonical mixed-endian 16-byte layout, exactly the same pattern we’ve used before: the first hyphen-group (8 hex digits) is written as a little-endian 32-bit integer, the second and third groups (4 hex digits each) are each written as a little-endian 16-bit integer, and the last two groups are written as raw bytes in big-endian order. Example stays exact: 01234567-0123-0123-0123-0123456789ab encodes to the 16 bytes 674523012301230101230123456789ab.

A couple rendering details too. Constants are resolved by name handle. float_scientific (e.g., 1.2345678e11) prints its numeric value in full decimal: 123456780000. Quoted string constants with embedded quotes and newlines must be rendered with proper JSON escape sequences: e.g., value="hello \"world\"\nwith newlines". The output is simply value=<rendered_value> on one line.

Also, byte arrays are the one array special case. An array of raw bytes (byte array) is encoded as the 4-byte little-endian element count followed by the contiguous raw bytes (one byte per element, no per-element framing). All other typed arrays (int16, int32, etc.) are encoded as the 4-byte count followed by each element individually encoded per its type's width. When we render decoded byte arrays, it should be [comma-separated decimal byte values].

On the 64-bit side, both int64 and uint64 are supplied in JSON as decimal strings so we don’t lose precision beyond JavaScript’s safe integer range, like "6" and "9223372036854775807". They still encode as 8 little-endian bytes. Decoded output renders them as plain decimal numbers in the text rendering, but for the 64-bit-backed enum size case the runtime kind needs to come through as 'bigint'.

And for the tagged message format, the shape is: a 4-byte little-endian prefix giving the byte length of the message body, then for each present field a 1-byte field number followed by that field's encoded value, then a terminating 0x00 byte. Absent fields are simply omitted. Decoding stops at the 0x00 terminator or at the declared body length. The body length counts all field-tag bytes, value bytes, and the terminator byte.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A discriminated union is encoded as: a 4-byte little-endian prefix giving the byte length of the union body, then a single 1-byte discriminator identifying the active branch, then that branch's payload encoded according to its own kind (structure or message). The discriminator value is the declared wire discriminator number, NOT the declaration order index. Non-sequential and out-of-order discriminators are fully supported.

In the decoded text rendering: plain structures/objects print their fields in ascending alphabetical order. Maps print their entries in wire order (the order they were encoded). Arrays print elements in order. Booleans print as true/false. Floats use Infinity, -Infinity, NaN for special values. Strings are JSON-quoted. Dates print as Date(<milliseconds>). GUIDs print as quoted hyphenated lowercase strings. Byte arrays print as [comma-separated decimal values].

Bit-flag enumeration members can be defined using bit shifts and bitwise combinations of earlier members. The values are: none=0, read=1 (1<<0), write=2 (1<<1), read_write=3 (read|write), something_else=4 (1<<2), complex=19 (read|write|something_else|eight... computed as bitwise OR of components), eight=8 (1<<3), minus_eight=-8. Combined members equal the bitwise OR of their named components. Negative values are supported.

Strings are encoded as a 4-byte little-endian unsigned byte-length prefix followed by the UTF-8 bytes of the string content. An empty string encodes as just 00000000 (4 zero bytes, no content bytes follow).

The decode_blob operation takes a hex-encoded binary blob and decodes it as a container holding a map from GUIDs to messages, where each message has: an optional string title (field 1), an optional uint16 year (field 2 based on test: value 0x07b6=1974), and an array of small fixed structures each pairing a name (string) with an enumeration value (uint32). Output format: songs_count=N, then for each entry: song.key=<guid>, song.title=<quoted string>, song.year=<int>, song.performers_count=N, song.performer[i]=<quoted name>:<enum_int>.

For large_array_roundtrip, the encoded_length equals 4 (the element-count prefix) plus 4 bytes per element (int32). So for 100000 elements: 4 + 100000*4 = 400004. Output five lines: encoded_length=<total bytes>, decoded_count=<count>, decoded_first=<first element>, decoded_last=<last element>, decoded_sum=<arithmetic sum of all elements as a plain integer>.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'decode_blob', 'scalars_roundtrip', 'arrays_roundtrip', 'large_array_roundtrip', 'maps_roundtrip', 'constant', 'flags', 'enum_sizes_roundtrip', 'union_roundtrip', 'union_reordered_roundtrip'. Each value maps to one feature.

Here are the public test cases for feature2_arrays_roundtrip:
{
  "description": "Round-trip a record whose fields are homogeneous arrays, one array per scalar element type. The byte array is encoded as a compact contiguous block; every other array is a length-prefixed sequence of its elements. The adapter encodes the record to wire bytes then decodes it back. The output reports the encoded bytes as hex and the decoded arrays, demonstrating element order and values are preserved for each element type.",

context_pointers.vague_reference: songs catalog format, I think it was spec'd somewhere in the test fixtures
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The decode_blob schema is implicitly defined by feature10_decode_blob.json: a map<guid, message> where each message has optional title (string, field tag 01), optional year (uint16, field tag 02 based on decoded value 1974=0x07b6), and an array of {name: string, role: uint32} fixed structs.

context_pointers.vague_reference: refer to how the existing round-trip tests expect the output to look
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The canonical output rendering rules are defined in the Wire Encoding Rules section of start.md under 'the rendering': alphabetical field order for structs, wire order for maps, JSON-quoting for strings, Date(<ms>) for dates, Infinity/-Infinity/NaN for special floats.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the bit-flag enum has some combined members and even a negative one
context_pointers.actual_meaning: feature6_flags.json specifies: none=0, read=1, write=2, read_write=3, something_else=4, complex=19, eight=8, minus_eight=-8. The complex member (19) is the bitwise OR of read(1)|write(2)|something_else(4)|eight(8)|... = 1|2|4|8|... = 15? No, 19=0b10011 = 16+2+1, so it includes an additional 1<<4=16 component beyond read|write|something_else|eight.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented in the ticket. Please check the test fixture files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the relevant feature, or refer to the Wire Encoding Rules section in start.md — those are the canonical sources of truth for the wire format contract.

feature10_decode_blob.json.description: Decode a pre-existing binary blob (supplied as a hex string) into a structured record: a container holding a map from globally-unique identifiers to messages, where each message has an optional title, an optional 16-bit year, and an array of small fixed structures each pairing a name with an enumeration value. The output reports the number of map entries and, for each entry, its key and the message's title, year, performer count, and each performer's name paired with its enumeration value.

cases.expected_output:
songs_count=1
song.key=81c6987b-48b7-495f-ad01-ec20cc5f5be1
song.title="Donna Lee"
song.year=1974
song.performers_count=2
song.performer[0]="Charlie Parker":0
song.performer[1]="Miles Davis":1

feature1_scalars_roundtrip.json.description: Round-trip a record made of one field of every scalar wire type (boolean, the four signed and unsigned fixed-width integers, both 64-bit integers, both floating-point widths, a length-prefixed UTF-8 string, a 16-byte globally-unique identifier rendered in canonical hyphenated form, and a date stored as an absolute instant). The adapter encodes the record to the wire byte sequence, then decodes that byte sequence back into a record. The output reports the encoded bytes as a lowercase hex string and the decoded field values, demonstrating that every scalar type survives an encode/decode cycle unchanged.

feature2_arrays_roundtrip.json.description: Round-trip a record whose fields are homogeneous arrays, one array per scalar element type. The byte array is encoded as a compact contiguous block; every other array is a length-prefixed sequence of its elements. The adapter encodes the record to wire bytes then decodes it back. The output reports the encoded bytes as hex and the decoded arrays, demonstrating element order and values are preserved for each element type.

feature3_large_array_roundtrip.json.description: Round-trip a record holding a single very large array of 32-bit integers built by repeating one value many times. Because emitting the full encoded blob and every decoded element would be unwieldy, the output instead reports the total encoded byte length, the decoded element count, the first and last decoded elements, and the arithmetic sum of all decoded elements. Together these prove the entire array is encoded and decoded without truncation or corruption.

feature4_maps_roundtrip.json.description: Round-trip a record exercising deeply nested associative-map structures: a map keyed by booleans, a map of maps, maps whose values are arrays of maps of small fixed structures, arrays of maps to arrays of floats, and a map keyed by a globally-unique identifier whose values are small messages. The adapter encodes the record then decodes it. The output reports the encoded bytes as hex and a deterministic textual rendering of every decoded map (entries shown in wire order), demonstrating that nested keyed containers round-trip intact.

feature5_constants.json.description: Resolve a compile-time constant declared in the schema and report its value. Constants cover signed and unsigned integers (including a large hexadecimal literal), a floating-point value written in scientific notation, the three special floating-point values (positive infinity, negative infinity, and not-a-number), both boolean literals, a string literal containing embedded quotes and a newline, and a globally-unique-identifier literal. Each request names one constant by a neutral handle; the output reports that constant's resolved value.

feature6_flags.json.description: Resolve the integer values of the members of a bit-flag enumeration whose members are defined using bit shifts and bitwise combinations of earlier members, including combined and negative members. Each request lists the member handles to resolve; the output reports each member handle paired with its resolved integer value, demonstrating that combined members equal the bitwise OR of their components.

cases.expected_output:
none=0
read=1
write=2
read_write=3
something_else=4
complex=19
eight=8
minus_eight=-8

feature7_enum_sizes_roundtrip.json.description: Round-trip a record containing two enumeration-typed fields with different backing-integer widths: one backed by a single unsigned byte and one backed by a signed 64-bit integer. The adapter encodes the record then decodes it. The output reports the encoded hex, the total encoded byte length (which reflects the sum of the two backing widths), each decoded field value, and the runtime numeric category of each decoded field (a plain number for the byte-backed enum and an arbitrary-precision integer for the 64-bit-backed enum), demonstrating that the larger field preserves full 64-bit range.

cases.expected_output:
encoded=7bffffffffffffff7f
encoded_length=9
decoded.small=123
decoded.small_kind=number
decoded.huge=9223372036854775807
decoded.huge_kind=bigint

feature8_union_roundtrip.json.description: Round-trip a discriminated union whose branches are selected by a one-based wire discriminator. The request supplies the discriminator and the payload for the corresponding branch; the adapter constructs the union, encodes it (a length prefix, then the discriminator byte, then the encoded branch payload), and decodes it back. The output reports the encoded hex, the decoded discriminator, and the decoded branch value, demonstrating the correct branch is recovered.

feature9_union_reordered_roundtrip.json.description: Round-trip a discriminated union whose declared branches use non-sequential, out-of-order wire discriminators (one branch reuses a higher number, one is skipped, and the lowest number is declared last). The request supplies the discriminator and the matching branch payload; the adapter constructs, encodes, and decodes the union. The output reports the encoded hex (whose discriminator byte equals the requested value regardless of declaration order) and the decoded discriminator and value, demonstrating that discriminator identity, not declaration order, governs the wire format.
